summary: Every activity row can change one process duration and therefore its machine's exact average; pairing and grouping the start/end streams matches this linear worst-case input lower bound.

optimality.argument: Changing the timestamp in any one of the R start or end rows can change a required processing_time value, so an exact algorithm has an Omega(R) worst-case input lower bound. A keyed join followed by grouped averaging processes the two event streams in O(R) expected work.

replacement_checks.evidence: Any activity timestamp may alter its process duration and its machine's returned average, establishing a linear worst-case input lower bound.
Filtering, joining on the composite machine-process key, and grouped aggregation require O(R) expected work with standard indexed or hash-based execution.
Authored fixtures cover the public sample, one-process machines, shared process IDs across machines, post-average rounding, nonconsecutive identifiers, and input row-order independence.
